coredata.dc:title: Serendipitous formation of photoluminescent carbon quantum dots by mere immersion of a polymer in an organic solvent
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Das, Saroj Kumar
Chakrabarty, Satadru
Gawas, Ramchandra
Jasuja, Kabeer
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are 0-dimensional carbon nanoparticles that display quantum confinement effects. Since their first discovery, an impressive library of CQD synthesis schemes has been developed. In this study we present a set of findings, that we came across serendipitously, that lead to an unintended formation of CQDs. We found, that a mere contact between a common polymeric material and an organic solvent resulted in a fluorescent dispersion. Detailed investigations revealed that the dispersion is made up of CQDs. Our analysis shows that these quantum dots exhibit primarily a blue fluorescence and are in the size range of 2–5 nm; these quantum dots are also nitrogen functionalized, and also show a good degree of crystallinity. Such a counterintuitive insight, to the best of our knowledge hasn't been reported earlier. Through this study we aim to bring this strange phenomenon to light and hope that these results would act as helpful inputs in the design of experiments.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Photoluminiscence
Excitation dependent emission
polymeric interactions

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid fundingbodyid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight oauserlicense openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder sponsoredaccesstype srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content oa subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast footnotes grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Insights into the formation of CQDs

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license.

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2022 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Formation of CQDs by interaction between ACN and the polymeric material of the O-ring a) bare ACN b) elastomer placed inside a vial of ACN c) UV irradiation of brownish dispersion d) HR-TEM image of a single CQD from the dispersion.

Structural and chemical characterization of the CQDs. (a) HRTEM image showing a CQD with size < 10 nm; (b) AFM image showing singular CQDs, the corresponding height profile in the inset indicates thickness of these QDs to be ∼2.5 nm (c) XRD pattern of CQDs with well-defined peaks centered around 21° and 45°, well in agreement with nitrogen containing as well as graphene-like QDs (d) High resolution XPS analysis showing C1s elemental spectrum and (e) N1s elemental spectrum clearly delineating the elemental constituents as C and N. Deconvolution of the XPS data showed that carbon is present mostly in graphitic form and the nitrogen present is in pyrolic as well as pyridinic forms (f) D band (1359 cm−1) and G band (1584 cm−1) corresponding to graphitic planes were observed by the Raman analysis of the CQDs. Also, the crystalline nature of the CQDs was ascertained by the ID/IG ratio.

Optical Properties of CQDs. (a) UV-visible absorption spectrum shows an intense peak at 270 nm, with two shoulders appearing at 372 and 450 nm respectively. The corresponding PL emission spectrum shows peak at 420 nm (b) Excitation wavelength dependent photoluminescence (c) The 2D excitation-emission map (d) Time-resolved fluorescence decay profile measured with excitation at 330 nm. The lifetime data and the parameters generated by the exponential fitting is shown in the inset.

Preliminary insights into the formation mechanism of CQDs. a) 1H NMR spectra clearly showing the extent of transformation in the re-dispersed CQDs when compared to that of pure ACN, that various species have formed is also evident from the presence of peaks in various ranges of chemical shift. b) FTIR spectra of pure and sheared ACN & re-dispersed CQDs. Appearance of new peaks in the 1500–700 cm−1 range indicate formation of new bonding states in the case of sheared ACN and re-dispersed CQDs.

head.ce:title: Serendipitous formation of photoluminescent carbon quantum dots by mere immersion of a polymer in an organic solvent

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Discipline of Chemical Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology, Palaj, Gandhinagar, Gujarat 382055, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Discipline of Chemical Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology, Palaj, Gandhinagar, Gujarat 382055, India

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are 0-dimensional carbon nanoparticles that display quantum confinement effects. Since their first discovery, an impressive library of CQD synthesis schemes has been developed. In this study we present a set of findings, that we came across serendipitously, that lead to an unintended formation of CQDs. We found, that a mere contact between a common polymeric material and an organic solvent resulted in a fluorescent dispersion. Detailed investigations revealed that the dispersion is made up of CQDs. Our analysis shows that these quantum dots exhibit primarily a blue fluorescence and are in the size range of 2–5 nm; these quantum dots are also nitrogen functionalized, and also show a good degree of crystallinity. Such a counterintuitive insight, to the best of our knowledge hasn't been reported earlier. Through this study we aim to bring this strange phenomenon to light and hope that these results would act as helpful inputs in the design of experiments.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots belong to the family of luminescent zero-dimensional nanoparticles, which also include noble metal nanoclusters, organic metal halide hybrids, semiconducting quantum dots etc. [1]. Carbon nanoparticles which are spherical, amorphous, and don't display quantum confinement effects are termed as carbon nanodots (CNDs), those which display quantum confinement and are spherical in nature, are referred to as carbon quantum dots (CQDs), whereas the pi-conjugated singular sheets are classified as graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [2].

CQDs were first synthesized during the preparative electrophoretic purification of single walled carbon nanotubes [3]. Since their discovery, CQDs have been synthesized via both top down and bottom-up approaches like many other classes of nanomaterials. Notable among these methods are— chemical and laser ablation [4,5], electrochemical carbonization [6], hydrothermal/solvothermal treatment [7], and microwave irradiation [8]. We acknowledge that an entire gamut of methods to synthesize CQDs has been developed over the years. Today, it is also possible to synthesize CQDs in large scale [9–11]. This work doesn't aim to add to the already existing library of CQD synthesis, albeit, in this work, we made a serendipitous observation— we found that CQDs can be formed by merely exposing a commonly used polymer to an organic solvent. In the subsequent sections, we apprise the reader in detail, on how we stumbled upon this odd observation.

Our research group's primary focus has been to exfoliate layered bulk materials into quasi two-dimensional (2D) nanostructures [12–16]. One of the widely used techniques for exfoliation is ultrasound assisted liquid phase exfoliation (LPE). It is while trying to synthesize 2D nanosheets by LPE of layered materials in organic solvents, we came across a consequence of ultrasound other than that of exfoliation. We showed that in addition to exfoliation, ultrasound also affects the liquid medium. The organic solvent used in the exfoliation process transforms into a dispersion of photoluminescent CQDs when subjected to prolonged ultrasonication [17].

Such a formation of CQDs driven only by ultrasound led us to envision- Can turbulent shear mixing also lead to a similar outcome? The rationale behind this proposition was that both ultrasonication and shear mixing are analogous in their energy outputs, as both these techniques are known to result in a similar outcome, i.e., exfoliation. Furthermore, such a possibility of synthesizing CQDs by the effect of turbulent mixing, would lead to a paradigm shift in this frontier by making QD synthesis widely accessible and inherently scalable. Remarkably, one doesn't require a specialized equipment to achieve effective high shear mixing rates; this can be achieved in a device as simple as a kitchen blender! Kitchen blenders have already found use in synthesizing nanomaterials [18–20]. Our working hypothesis was- high shear mixing of an organic solvent would lead to its transformation to CQDs through a quasi-mechanochemical route. However, the actual findings from our experiments were completely baffling.

We observed during our experimentation, that the organic solvent when in contact with the O-ring of the blender jar (a common elastomeric material), was able to react with the material in such a way, that led to the formation of a brownish dispersion. This dispersion showed Tyndall effect, which led us to believe that a colloidal species must be present. Intrigued, we analyzed this dispersion thoroughly. Our investigations show that a perceived dissolution of the elastomeric material in the organic solvent, leads to the formation of carbonaceous species much alike CQDs, that exhibit excitation wavelength dependent emission. These CQDs were of ∼2–3 nanometers in dimension. XPS analysis showed that these CQDs primarily consist of carbon and nitrogen. Another interesting finding was that of the crystalline nature of the CQDs as determined through XRD and Raman spectroscopy results.

Although we obtained CQDs, but they were not from high shear mixing, as we had hypothesized. Rather, it was due to a hitherto unexplored interaction between a polymeric material and an organic solvent. We believe that the interaction of a polymeric material with an organic solvent, leading to the formation of fluorescent CQDs hasn't been reported, if at all considered. Thus, through this study we aim to bring into notice this strange possibility, and also urge the community to err on the side of caution while designing experiments with common household devices, as these devices can yield results that might muddle the correct interpretation of scientific outcomes.

Our initial experimentation involved the high shear mixing of an organic solvent, acetonitrile (ACN) inside a kitchen blender. After conducting such an experiment, we found that the appearance of ACN had changed from colorless to brownish yellow. This at first, indicated that perhaps shear mixing was indeed causing a transformation. However, as a part of negative control experiments, where we tried to rule out the possibility of any component from the device contributing to the experiment, we were surprised.

We noticed when the O-ring material was allowed to come in contact with ACN, there was a definite color change that took place (Fig. 1 ). This conveyed that probably high shear mixing in and of itself wasn't transforming ACN. This finding pointed towards the possibility of the elastomer reacting in some way with ACN. In the subsequent sections, we will show that the interaction between the elastomer (which is most likely made of neoprene rubber, see ESI section I) and ACN results in the formation of luminescent CQDs. It was also seen that not only this particular O-ring, but ACN interacts with a different O-ring in a similar fashion. Furthermore, to ascertain if impurities present on the O-ring surface, we plasma cleaned the O-ring before immersing it in ACN. Even after the cleaning procedure, we obtained a brownish ACN dispersion which fluoresced after coming in contact with the O-ring (See ESI section II). To prove further that there is a definite interaction between the polymer and acetonitrile, we procured pure neoprene and repeated the experiments. A brownish dispersion was formed which also exhibited fluorescence, indicating that pure polymers can react in a similar way with acetonitrile (See ESI section II, Figure S4).

The brownish-yellow liquid that we obtained exhibited Tyndall effect, indicating the formation of a dispersed phase (Fig. S5). Interestingly, upon irradiation with UV light, the colloidal dispersion displayed a bright blue fluorescence. ACN doesn't exhibit any native photoluminescence. We analysed this dispersed phase under HR-TEM, and found it to contain nanostructures with dimensions <10 nm (Fig. 2 a). The lattice fringes exhibited by these nanostructures are distinct from the amorphous substrate background. We found that these nanostructures have a mean size of ∼3 nm (Fig. S7), hence we refer to these as quantum dots (QDs). The line profiles from the HRTEM images reveal that the lattice spacing of these QDs is ∼0.21 nm (Fig. S6 d) which corresponds to the (100) plane of graphitic carbon [21–23]. The well-resolved lattice fringes in HRTEM images indicate they are crystalline (more HR-TEM images are shown in figure S6). Such a bottom-up formation of crystalline QDs by a mere dissolution of a polymeric material is surprising and probably overlooked. The height profile obtained from the AFM image of the QDs indicate their thickness in the range of 2–3 nm (Fig. 2b, & Fig. S9).

The crystallinity of the QDs is further evinced by the XRD pattern, shown in Fig. 2c. The XRD profile shows a strong peak centered around 21° which corresponds to the (002) plane of graphite-like structures [24–27]. Similarly, an additional peak at ∼45° is assigned to the (101) graphitic plane [28,29]. These attributes suggest that the synthesized QDs have a chemical identity similar to the carbon/graphene quantum dots (CQDs/GQDs). Usually, the XRD patterns reported for most QDs exhibit broad and diffused peaks owing to their amorphous nature. [30,31] In contrast, the XRD pattern of the QDs synthesized by shear mixing exhibits sharp peaks indicating they are highly crystalline. This observation is also unique compared to our earlier study wherein ultrasonication of ACN produced amorphous quantum dots [17]. We recognize that further investigations will be needed to probe the exact cause of crystallinity of the CQDs. It can however be a result of not employing harsh reaction conditions in the synthesis of the CQDs, which is usually the case in many of the widely available techniques [23,32]. Reports have suggested that it is possible to form nanocrystalline core at room temperatures to synthesize CQDs [33,34]. Thus, we believe that the interaction between the polymer and solvent is occurring in such a way that leads to the formation of a crystalline graphite core which ultimately leads to the formation of the CQDs.

We also investigated the chemical composition of these CQDs by XPS (since the TEM grids are primarily made of carbon film, we avoided EDX analysis as it could possibly interfere with the correct estimation of elements). Although the major constituent of the CQDs is found to be carbon (98%), we also observed the presence of nitrogen (2%) (Fig. S8, Table S2). Thus, we termed them as nitrogen-containing CQDs. The de-convoluted C1s spectrum (Fig. 2d) showed two daughter peaks at 284.7 and 285.5 eV attributed to CC/C = C, and CN/CH bonds respectively [22,24,35–37]. Similarly, the de-convoluted N1s spectrum revealed four daughter peaks (Fig. 2e) at 398.5, 399.4, 400.5, and 402.1 eV. While the first two peaks correspond to pyridinic N centres, the latter two can be assigned to pyrrolic and graphitic nitrogen species [35,38].

Further information about the nature of chemical bonding in these CQDs was obtained by Raman spectroscopy. We observed two distinct peaks (Fig. 2f) - (i) The 1359 cm−1 peak, represents sp3 hybridized carbon (D-band), (ii) the 1584 cm−1 peak can be attributed to the in-plane vibration of sp2 hybridized carbon (G-band) [22,39,40]. In addition, a small Raman peak is also evident around ∼1450 cm−1 which is ascribed to the presence of pyridinic or pyrrolic nitrogen in the CQDs [41], and agrees well with our XPS results. The ratio of disordered to ordered carbon (ID/IG) was calculated as 0.71, signifying high percentage of sp2 carbon atoms in the CQDs. This correlates with the highly crystalline nature of CQDs suggested by XRD analysis [42,43].

We then studied the optical properties of these CQDs. Fig.3 a shows the UV–Vis spectrum and the PL emission exhibited by the CQDs. The UV–Vis signature shows the presence of a strong peak at ∼270 nm, a shoulder peak at ∼330 nm, and a small peak at ∼430 nm. While the first peak arises from the sp2 carbon atoms of the CQDs (π→π* transition), the second and third peak are an outcome of the nitrogen-containing functional groups (n→σ* & n→π* transitions, respectively) [23,44]. These CQDs exhibited a strong PL, the intensity of which depends on the excitation wavelength. Fig. 3b represents the excitation and emission spectrum of these CQDs – the excitation maximum is located at ∼310 nm, the emission maximum is located at ∼360 nm. When the excitation wavelengths were varied from 250 to 410 nm, the emission peaks were red shifted from ∼300 to ∼450 nm. This is also evident from the 2D excitation-emission map (Fig. 3c). Fluorescence decay profile is depicted in Fig. 3d. The data was fitted to a triple exponential decay function. The decay profile can be seen in inset. We found these CQDs have an average lifetime of 4.9 ns. The quantum yield of CQDs was calculated to be ∼16% (Table S3). We also investigated the effect of mixing time on the optical properties of the CQDs, and found that with increasing mixing time, the absorption and emission intensities also correspondingly increased (Fig. S10). This suggests that the reaction between ACN and the elastomer intensifies with passing time, resulting in a concentrated dispersion of CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Insights into the formation of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain initial insights into what these species could be, we carried out FTIR and NMR analysis of ACN before and after shear mixing (Fig. 4 ). It can be discerned that indeed a transformation has taken place. NMR analysis of pure acetonitrile and re-dispersed CQDs also demonstrate marked changes (Fig. 4a). While the spectrum of pure ACN is almost featureless, that of the re-dispersed CQDs shows a collection of peaks. This led us to claim that the reactive species formed due to the reaction between the O-ring and ACN have undergone polymerization, condensation and carbonization to ultimately form CQDs [1,45]. This also insinuates that the polymeric part of the O-ring is primarily the source for forming the CQDs. We have tried to capture this transformation by FT-IR analysis as well. Appearance of newer peaks in case of acetonitrile after interaction with the polymeric material and re-dispersed CQDs indicate that new bond formations have taken place compared to the pure ACN FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 4b). Organic solvents are known to react with and degrade materials made of low-grade plastic. But even with the relatively better stability of O-ring materials with ACN, we found that indeed that's not the case, evident from the brownish dispersion formed which we confirmed as CQDs through detailed analysis. We however do acknowledge that further studies need to be undertaken to reveal the exact formation mechanism and whether the carbon source is from the polymeric material or the filler-like materials that are generally added to such polymers.

In conclusion, we have shown here, how an unwanted interaction between a polymeric material and an organic solvent result in a dispersion of fluorescent carbon quantum dots. Initially, we had sought to synthesize CQDs by mere high shear mixing of an organic solvent in a household kitchen blender which would have been a significant addition to the vast range of methods employed for synthesizing CQDs. Even though we were unsuccessful in attaining our primary objective, the very fact that CQDs can be obtained by mere contact between a polymer and an organic solvent is truly remarkable. We believe that an organic solvent reacts adversely with the polymeric material of the O-ring of the blender jar, which is the primary cause for the CQD formation. More in-depth investigations would be required to properly delineate this phenomenon. This report thus simultaneously throws light on a new chemical interaction while also serving as a counsel against the usage of non-specific equipment in academic research settings.

Acetonitrile (HPLC grade, ≥99.9%) was procured from Sigma Aldrich. The O-ring was taken from an off the shelf kitchen blender (Bajaj, Bravo Dlx 750). Another O-ring was obtained from a different kitchen blender (Bajaj, Pluto 500 W). Pure neoprene chunks (Polychloroprene, 85% trans, 15% cis) was obtained from Sigma Aldrich. All the polymeric materials were plasma cleaned before experimentation

In a typical experiment, a portion of the O-ring was cut and put into ∼15 mL of ACN in a glass vial. The vial was placed in dark left undisturbed for 60 mins. After, the completion of 60 min, the brownish colored suspension was collected and filtered (PVDF membrane, 0.2 μm). This filtered suspension was then used for further analysis. No visible physical change to the O-ring was observed. To check whether impurities are playing a role in the polymer-solvent interaction we plasma cleaned the O-ring to remove any trace impurities and repeated the experiments to obtain similar results, experiments with pure neoprene was carried out in a similar manner (details included in ESI section II).

Samples for TEM analysis was prepared by drop casting a filtered aliquot of the shear mixed acetonitrile onto a pure carbon coated copper grid (Ted Pella, 300 mesh, 3 mm diameter). Images were obtained using JEOL JEM-2200 FS transmission electron microscope (Japan), operated at 200 kV. The analysis of the images was carried out by GATAN digital micrograph software.

Analysis was carried out on a Laser Raman Spectrometer (Airix Corp, STR500, Japan), the spectrum was recorded using 532 nm excitation laser. The objective lens was set at 50x with a laser power of 50 mW. Sample was prepared on a cleaned SiO2 coated Si wafer substrate by drop casting method.

XPS data was recorded in AXIS Supra (Kratos Analytical, Shimadzu group, UK) using a monochromatic Al-Kα source (600 W). To calibrate the peak positions, the binding energy of silicon was used as an internal standard. Prior to analysis, the sample was drop casted onto a SiO2- coated Si wafer and dried in a vacuum desiccator for 24 h to completely remove any traces of solvent.

The thickness of CQDs was obtained by AFM (Bruker, Multimode, 8 AM). The microscope was operated in ScanAsyst tapping mode. To prepare the sample, firstly a SiO2 coated silicon wafer of approximately 1 cm x 1 cm was cleaned and dried, then a 10μL CQD dispersion was spin coated on to the wafer (2500 rpm, 30 s). The spin coated sample was then vacuum dried prior to the actual analysis in a desiccator to completely remove any residual solvent. The images were obtained with the aid of a silicon cantilever (spring constant 0.4 N/m and resonating frequency 70 kHz). The NanoScope software package was used to analyze the AFM images.

The XRD analysis was carried out on a Bruker D8 Discover Diffractometer (USA) using Cu Kα radiation (40 kV, 30 mA). The scan was performed with a step size of 0.02° and a scan speed of 0.2 s/step. A drop of the CQD dispersion was deposited onto a freshly cleaned glass slide and then allowed to vacuum dry to completely remove the solvent. The deposited sample was then mounted onto a circular disk shaped sample holder.

The UV–visible absorption spectra of the shear mixed samples were recorded in UV-1700 (Shimadzu, Pharma Spec UV–Vis) in the range 200–900 nm in a quartz cuvette, having a path length of 10 mm. All spectra were recorded at room temperature. Pure acetonitrile was used as a reference/background.

The fluorescence spectra of the shear mixed samples were measured in a spectro-fluorometer (Fluorolog Horiba Jobin Yvon, USA). The contour map for the excitation-emission behavior was prepared using MATLAB.

The 1H NMR experiments were performed in CDCl3 with tetramethyl silane (TMS) as an internal standard using Bruker Advance III 500 MHz NMR spectrometer. The sample preparation was done in two ways, firstly, 20μL of the as-obtained shear mixed acetonitrile was added to 600μL of CDCl3 in an NMR tube and was then analyzed. In the second method, shear mixed acetonitrile was allowed to evaporate leaving behind a brown residue which was then dispersed in 600μL of CDCl3, and taken for analysis, in the latter method we obtained a more concentrated dispersion of the CQDs enabling us to visualize the changes that shear mixing caused.

The FTIR spectrum was recorded by Perkin Elmer Spectrum 2 spectrometer using attenuated total reflection mode in the range of 400–4000 cm−1. A drop of shear mixed acetonitrile as well as re-dispersed CQDs (prepared similarly as for NMR experiments) was drop casted on the FTIR detection probe and allowed to dry completely before collecting the spectrum.

The O-ring portions were plasma cleaned to remove any surface impurities that could have interfered with the interaction between the solvent and the polymeric material. The cleaning was carried out by Argon-Oxygen plasma in a Gatan Solarus Plasma Cleaning System, model:955, for 10 min.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by seed funding from IIT Gandhinagar, Core Research Grant (EMR/2017/000730) by the Science and Engineering Research Board, Fast Track Research Grant for Young Scientists (SB/FTP/ETA-114/2013), and INSPIRE Faculty Award Research Grant (DST/INSPIRE/04/2014/001601) by the Department of Science and Technology, India. Raman and XPS studies were conducted at IIT Kanpur, India. We appreciate the help of Dr. Manish Shinde (CMET, Pune, India) for the help with TEM and HR-TEM imaging. We deeply acknowledge the help extended by Palash Jana, Beena Kumari (with lifetime analysis and NMR experiments), Pradeep Yadav, Ajay Mohan (with XRD analysis), Shivam Shukla (with AFM analysis), Jay Shah (with back-end experiments) We are grateful to Prof. Sriram Kanvah and Prof. Sharad Gupta who provided valuable insights about the analyses. We also thank the IIT Gandhinagar Central Instrumentation Facility for providing access to various instruments during this research.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.cartre.2022.100183.
